region_of_origin: Sweden (Oatly, founded 1994); widely available in Singapore supermarkets including Cold Storage, FairPrice, and Redmart
food_subgroup: Non-dairy milk alternative — fortified oat-based beverage; commercial, sweetened or unsweetened, with added calcium, vitamin D, and vitamin B12; 250 ml serving

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for commercial fortified oat milk. Closest parent concept is oat-based beverage. URI not assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0P0C used as closest parent for oat-based fluid drink. No exact code for commercial fortified oat milk exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for commercial fortified oat milk beverage. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

preparation_method: fortification with calcium, vitamin D, vitamin B12
allergens: gluten (oats — not suitable for coeliac without certified gluten-free label)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard 250 ml glass of commercial fortified oat milk, equivalent to a typical single-serve carton or one cup used in café coffee drinks.

nutrient_source.title: Oat Milk (Commercial, Fortified) — Oatly Barista Product Label + USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Oatly Singapore product label; USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values represent a composite of Oatly Barista Edition Singapore product label and USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods entries for fortified oat milk. Per-100g values are consistent across major commercial brands (Oatly, Minor Figures, Meiji Oat Milk) within ±10%. Significant brand-to-brand variability exists, particularly for calcium (96–120 mg/100 ml), vitamin D (1.5–3 mcg/100 ml), and vitamin B12 (0.4–0.8 mcg/100 ml). Always verify against specific product label.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Oat Milk — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on oat-based beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed GI literature (Wolever et al.; Brand-Miller et al.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of oat milk is reported in the range of 60–70 in the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature. The elevated GI (vs. cow's milk GI ~27–34 or soy milk GI ~34) is attributable to maltose generated during enzymatic hydrolysis of oat starch. GI value of 65 used as central estimate. GL per 250 ml serving calculated as (65 × 16 g available carbohydrate per 250 ml) / 100 = 10.4; input record reports 7.8 based on 12 g available carbohydrate per serving, which is consistent with lower-carbohydrate formulations. GL of 7.8 retained from verified source input.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 175 mg per 250 ml serving
hypertension.guidance: Oat milk contributes approximately 175 mg sodium per 250 ml serving — moderate and comparable to cow's milk (~120 mg per 250 ml) — and is generally acceptable for hypertensive patients within a balanced diet, though individuals on strict sodium restriction should check specific brand labels as sodium content varies.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic index ~65 (medium-high) driven by maltose from enzymatic oat starch hydrolysis
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oat milk has a GI of approximately 65 — substantially higher than cow's whole milk (~30) or soy milk (~34) — due to maltose generated during enzymatic processing; T2DM patients should not assume oat milk is a low-GI dairy alternative, and the beta-glucan content in liquid form is insufficient to meaningfully blunt postprandial glucose response; glycemic load per 250 ml is approximately 7.8, which is moderate but higher than cow's milk (~3.3).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (1.0 g per 250 ml), zero cholesterol, and no trans fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oat milk is low in saturated fat (approximately 1.0 g per 250 ml) and contains zero cholesterol and zero trans fat, making it a heart-friendly dairy alternative; however, the beta-glucan content in commercial liquid oat milk is insufficient to achieve the 3 g/day threshold associated with clinically meaningful LDL cholesterol reduction, so cardiovascular benefit from beta-glucan should not be assumed.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin D fortification (~5 mcg per 250 ml) and calcium (~240 mg per 250 ml) relevant for RA bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vitamin D fortification in commercial oat milk (~5 mcg per 250 ml, representing 25–50% of RDA) is clinically relevant for RA patients, in whom vitamin D deficiency is common and correlates with disease activity; calcium content (~240 mg per 250 ml) also supports bone health in RA patients at elevated osteoporosis risk from corticosteroid use.

coeliac_disease.key_factor: Oats contain gluten; standard commercial oat milk is not certified gluten-free
coeliac_disease.guidance: Standard commercial oat milk is not suitable for coeliac disease patients unless the product carries a certified gluten-free label, as oats contain avenin (a gluten-related protein) and are frequently cross-contaminated with wheat during processing.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oat Milk (Commercial Fortified, 250 ml serving) is a plant-based, dairy-free beverage made by blending oats with water, followed by enzymatic hydrolysis of oat starch to produce a smooth, mildly sweet liquid. The enzymatic process converts oat starch into maltose and shorter-chain sugars, which gives oat milk its characteristic mild sweetness and also elevates its glycemic index to approximately 65 — substantially higher than cow's whole milk (GI ~30) or soy milk (GI ~34). Commercial fortified oat milk sold in Singapore (brands: Oatly, Minor Figures, Meiji Oat Milk) is enriched with calcium (~96–120 mg/100 ml), vitamin D (~2–3 mcg/100 ml), and vitamin B12 (~0.4–0.8 mcg/100 ml), making it nutritionally closer to cow's milk than unfortified oat milk, which contains negligible calcium (~15 mg/100 ml). Per 100 ml: approximately 52 kcal, 1.4 g protein, 3.0 g fat (0.4 g saturated), 6.4 g carbohydrate (2.8 g sugar), 0.8 g dietary fibre, 70 mg sodium. Per 250 ml serving: approximately 130 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 7.5 g fat, 16 g carbohydrate, 175 mg sodium, 240 mg calcium, 5 mcg vitamin D, 1.5 mcg vitamin B12. Texture is creamy and slightly viscous, with a mild oat flavour that pairs well with coffee and tea. Widely used in Singapore specialty cafés as a dairy alternative in flat whites and lattes. CLINICAL NOTES: (1) GI is significantly higher than perceived — oat milk is NOT a low-GI dairy alternative; T2DM patients should account for this. (2) Beta-glucan content in liquid form is insufficient for cholesterol or glucose benefit. (3) Fortification varies enormously between brands — always verify the product label. (4) Contains gluten — not suitable for coeliac disease without a certified gluten-free label. (5) Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free diets. Source: Oatly Singapore product label + USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods. Confidence: medium (significant brand variability).
meal_context: café coffee milk alternative (flat white, latte, cappuccino)

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from Oatly Singapore product label and USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, both approved sources. However, significant brand-to-brand variability exists for key fortified nutrients (calcium, vitamin D, vitamin B12), and values may differ by up to ±20% across commercial products. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database range (60–70) rather than a single exact product measurement. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: CRITICAL BRAND VARIABILITY WARNING: Nutrient values — especially calcium, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 — vary significantly between brands and product lines. Unfortified oat milk contains negligible calcium (~15 mg/100 ml). Always verify against the specific product label. GLUTEN ALERT: Standard commercial oat milk is not gluten-free and is not suitable for coeliac disease patients without a certified GF label. GI NOTE: Oat milk GI (~65) is substantially higher than cow's milk (~30) or soy milk (~34) due to maltose from enzymatic hydrolysis; the beta-glucan content in liquid form is insufficient to blunt postprandial glucose response. Cholesterol is confirmed zero — plant-based product contains no animal-derived ingredients. Vitamin C is confirmed zero per product label — not added in standard formulations. Trans fat is confirmed zero per product label. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are not reported in the source data and are marked as -1.0. Water content is not reported in the source data and is marked as -1.0.
